tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
 - VROUNDSD: 4 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_1>>
hint.title: Complex instructions
Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt: 17 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your function (in the source code) and the binary function
hint.txt:
The binary function is composed of 19 FP arithmetical operations:
 - 6: addition or subtraction (all inside FMA instructions)
 - 13: multiply (6 inside FMA instructions)
The binary function is loading 40 bytes (5 double precision FP elements).
The binary function is storing 8 bytes (1 double precision FP elements).

hint.title: Arithmetic intensity
hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.40 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

expert.title: General properties
expert.txt:
nb instructions    : 24
nb uops            : 27
loop length        : 123
used x86 registers : 1
used mmx registers : 0
used xmm registers : 6
used ymm registers : 0
used zmm registers : 0
nb stack references: 0

expert.help: Front-end corresponds to instructions fetching and decoding into macro/micro operations
expert.title: Front-end
expert.txt:
MACRO FUSION NOT POSSIBLE
FIT IN UOP CACHE
micro-operation queue: 6.75 cycles
front end            : 6.75 cycles

expert.help: Back-end corresponds to macro/micro operations execution (includes allocating registers, fetching operands etc.)
expert.title: Back-end
expert.txt:
| P0    | P1    | P2   | P3   | P4   | P5   | P6   | P7
----------------------------------------------------------------
uops   | 10.50 | 10.50 | 2.50 | 2.50 | 1.00 | 0.00 | 1.00 | 2.00
cycles | 10.50 | 10.50 | 2.50 | 2.50 | 1.00 | 0.00 | 1.00 | 2.00

Execution ports to units layout:
 - P0 (256 bits): VPU, BRU, ALU, DIV/SQRT
 - P1 (256 bits): ALU, VPU
 - P2 (256 bits): store address, load
 - P3 (256 bits): store address, load
 - P4 (256 bits): store data
 - P5: ALU
 - P6: ALU, BRU
 - P7: store address

Cycles executing div or sqrt instructions: NA

expert.txt:

add-sub : NA (no add-sub vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
fma     : 0%
div/sqrt: NA (no div/sqrt vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
other   : 0%

expert.help: Effective/average relative length/width used in vector/SIMD registers. Higher is better.
expert.title: Vector efficiency ratios
expert.txt:
all     : 25%
load    : 25%
store   : 25%
mul     : 25%
add-sub : NA (no add-sub vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
fma     : 25%
div/sqrt: NA (no div/sqrt vectorizable/vectorized instructions)
other   : 25%

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 10.50 cycles. At this rate:
 - 5% of peak load performance is reached (3.81 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 2% of peak store performance is reached (0.76 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.help: List of bottlenecks detected by CQA. A bottleneck is an issue which reduces front-end throughput below the core capacity.
expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks

header:
Warnings:
The number of fused uops of the instruction [ENDBR64] is unknown
11% of peak computational performance is used (1.81 out of 16.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Make array accesses unit-stride:
  * If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA)
gain.details:
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is not vectorized.
4 data elements could be processed at once in vector registers.
<<image_1x64_256>>By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 10.50 to 2.62 cycles (4.00x speedup).

gain.workaround: Reduce the number of FP multiply/FMA instructions
gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt:
Performance is limited by execution of FP multiply or FMA (fused multiply-add) operations (the FP multiply/FMA unit is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 10.50 to 6.75 cycles (1.56x speedup).
